name: domain-fusion-materials
description: Use web_search to search for 'fusion reactor materials tungsten first wall blanket neutron damage'. Results will overlap with earlier searches. What are the key materials challenges for fusion reactors? Discuss first-wall materials and neutron damage. Keep response under 100 words.

ground_truth.reference_answer: The first wall and divertor of a fusion reactor face extreme conditions: neutron fluxes of ~10^14 n/cm²/s, heat loads of 2 MW/m² on the blanket and 10-20 MW/m² on the divertor, and cumulative radiation damage exceeding 100 displacements per atom (dpa) in commercial plants. Tungsten is the leading plasma-facing material due to its high melting point (3,422°C) and low sputtering rate, but it suffers from neutron-induced embrittlement as its ductile-to-brittle transition temperature rises under irradiation. No existing material has been demonstrated to survive commercial fusion conditions long-term. Research into tungsten-fiber composites, self-passivating tungsten alloys (with chromium and titanium), and advanced ceramics like MAX phases is underway to address these challenges.
key_facts: Tungsten is the leading plasma-facing material due to high melting point and low sputtering
Neutron-induced embrittlement raises tungsten's ductile-to-brittle transition temperature
Commercial fusion plants will subject materials to over 100 dpa radiation damage
Divertor heat loads can reach 10-20 MW/m²
No existing material has been demonstrated to survive full commercial fusion conditions
